← Neurology
Neurology 30 September 2025

Long-Term Potentiation: How Synapses Learn

Memory has to be written somewhere. Long-term potentiation is the leading candidate for the physical act of writing — a lasting strengthening of the connection between two neurons that fire together. This paper follows the molecular sequence that turns a moment of coincident activity into a durable change in the brain.

The Axon 2 min read

In 1949 the psychologist Donald Hebb proposed a rule so compact it now reads like a slogan: neurons that fire together, wire together. Long-term potentiation, or LTP, is the closest thing neuroscience has found to that rule made flesh.

A gate that counts coincidences

The story centres on the synapse — the junction where one neuron passes a signal to the next — and on a particular receptor for the neurotransmitter glutamate: the NMDA receptor. This receptor is a coincidence detector. It opens only when two things happen at once: the sending neuron releases glutamate, and the receiving neuron is already depolarised from its own recent activity.

That double condition is the whole trick. A magnesium ion normally plugs the channel; only when the receiving cell is sufficiently excited does the plug pop free, letting calcium flood in. Calcium is the signal that says: these two cells were active together — record it.

From a pulse to a lasting change

The calcium influx sets off a cascade. Enzymes such as CaMKII are activated, and they drive more AMPA receptors into the receiving membrane. With more receptors listening, the same amount of glutamate now produces a larger response. The synapse has been potentiated — strengthened — and it stays that way.

Sustained potentiation recruits a second phase. New proteins are synthesised, and the synapse is physically remodelled: the contact grows, sometimes splitting into two. What began as a fleeting electrical coincidence has become a change in structure.

Early LTP adjusts the sensitivity of an existing connection. Late LTP rebuilds the connection itself. The transition from one to the other is, in miniature, the transition from holding something in mind to remembering it.

The limits of the metaphor

It is tempting to call LTP “the memory molecule,” but the honest claim is narrower. LTP is a mechanism by which synaptic strength can be durably changed, demonstrated most cleanly in the hippocampus, a structure essential to forming new memories. Whether a given memory is stored as a particular pattern of potentiated synapses remains a question at the frontier.

What LTP does give us is a proof of principle: experience can leave a lasting, molecular trace at the exact junctions where neurons talk. The brain does not merely process information. It rewrites itself in response to it.

More in Neurology

N 2 min
Neurology 14 Nov 2025

The Myelin Sheath and the Speed of Thought

Myelin is the nervous system's answer to a physics problem: how to move a signal quickly along a thin, leaky biological wire. This paper traces how the insulating sheath and the gaps between its segments turn a slow, decaying current into a fast, self-renewing pulse — and what happens when the insulation fails.

The Axon
N 2 min
Neurology 22 Jul 2025

The Blood–Brain Barrier as a Selective Gate

The brain is bathed in blood yet walled off from it. The blood–brain barrier is the wall — a living membrane that admits fuel and blocks intruders. This paper examines how the barrier is built, why the brain needs one, and why it is the central obstacle in treating neurological disease.
